APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an in-scope topic for a top NLP/ML venue: interactive semantic parsing and language acquisition for natural language interfaces, using a realistic Voxelurn deployment and comparisons to prior interactive supervision (Wang et al. 2016). The problem formulation (bootstrapping a core programming language via user definitions and grammar induction) and the alignment-based chart extension are appropriate and of interest to the community. My main concern for full suitability is methodological completeness: evaluation relies on usage statistics (240 structures, 74.7% naturalized utterances) but lacks reported code, hyperparameters, seeds, and more rigorous quantitative/ablation analyses to fully assess generality and reproducibility. Overall this is a strong fit but would benefit from additional experimental rigor to meet top-tier expectations. | B(5): This paper addresses core topics in NLP/ML—semantic parsing, grammar induction, and interactive learning for natural-language programming—and is directly relevant to this community by extending SEMPRE and proposing an alignment-based chart extension. The methodological approach (interactive definition head–body pairs, conservative rule induction) and evaluation with a community building 240 voxel structures and a large fraction (74.7%) of utterances using the naturalized language provide the kind of empirical and usage-driven evidence expected at a top venue. Limitations are acknowledged (scalability of candidate selection, conservative paraphrase induction), indicating appropriate positioning and realistic claims for the NLP/ML audience.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an in-scope topic for a top NLP/ML venue: interactive semantic parsing and language acquisition for natural language interfaces, using a realistic Voxelurn deployment and comparisons to prior interactive supervision (Wang et al. 2016). The problem formulation (bootstrapping a core programming language via user definitions and grammar induction) and the alignment-based chart extension are appropriate and of interest to the community. My main concern for full suitability is methodological completeness: evaluation relies on usage statistics (240 structures, 74.7% naturalized utterances) but lacks reported code, hyperparameters, seeds, and more rigorous quantitative/ablation analyses to fully assess generality and reproducibility. Overall this is a strong fit but would benefit from additional experimental rigor to meet top-tier expectations.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses core topics in NLP/ML—semantic parsing, grammar induction, and interactive learning for natural-language programming—and is directly relevant to this community by extending SEMPRE and proposing an alignment-based chart extension. The methodological approach (interactive definition head–body pairs, conservative rule induction) and evaluation with a community building 240 voxel structures and a large fraction (74.7%) of utterances using the naturalized language provide the kind of empirical and usage-driven evidence expected at a top venue. Limitations are acknowledged (scalability of candidate selection, conservative paraphrase induction), indicating appropriate positioning and realistic claims for the NLP/ML audience.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles a relevant problem—making programming-language-like interfaces more natural via interactive definition and grammar induction—and demonstrates practical uptake (community-built 240 complex voxel structures and 74.7% usage in the last 10K utterances), which suggests useful practical value. However, the technique is conservative (alignment-based chart extension only for single-utterance bodies) and has clear scalability/usability limitations for large action spaces, and the evidence is limited to one domain (Voxelurn) with few reported experimental controls (no hyperparameters/code noted). Overall this is a solid, enabling contribution that will motivate follow-up work but is not yet transformative across the wider NL-interface field. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—making formal programming interfaces more natural for end users—by demonstrating a community-driven interactive naturalization method that achieved substantial uptake (74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and supported creation of 240 complex voxel structures. Its chart-extension and conservative grammar induction approach shows a realistic path for scalable paraphrase generalization and highlights clear usability trade-offs (precision vs. generalization), which should influence how researchers design interactive semantic parsers. The work opens promising directions for community-driven language evolution and human-in-the-loop API extension, and its demonstrated real-world usage makes it likely to be built upon by both NLP and HCI practitioners.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a relevant problem—making programming-language-like interfaces more natural via interactive definition and grammar induction—and demonstrates practical uptake (community-built 240 complex voxel structures and 74.7% usage in the last 10K utterances), which suggests useful practical value. However, the technique is conservative (alignment-based chart extension only for single-utterance bodies) and has clear scalability/usability limitations for large action spaces, and the evidence is limited to one domain (Voxelurn) with few reported experimental controls (no hyperparameters/code noted). Overall this is a solid, enabling contribution that will motivate follow-up work but is not yet transformative across the wider NL-interface field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—making formal programming interfaces more natural for end users—by demonstrating a community-driven interactive naturalization method that achieved substantial uptake (74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and supported creation of 240 complex voxel structures. Its chart-extension and conservative grammar induction approach shows a realistic path for scalable paraphrase generalization and highlights clear usability trade-offs (precision vs. generalization), which should influence how researchers design interactive semantic parsers. The work opens promising directions for community-driven language evolution and human-in-the-loop API extension, and its demonstrated real-world usage makes it likely to be built upon by both NLP and HCI practitioners.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (interactive definitions, alignment-based chart extension, community results), making the flow from method to experiments easy to follow. Notation and examples appear consistent and the writing quality is good, with effective figures/tables implied by the clear reporting of user-facing metrics (240 structures, 74.7% naturalized utterances). Presentation could be improved by explicitly reporting implementation details and hyperparameters (code availability is not mentioned) and clarifying the conservative alignment induction conditions in more detail to aid reproducibility. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusion, and its main contributions are stated early (interactive definitions + alignment-based chart extension). Notation appears consistent and the examples (voxel structures, user statistics) and figures/tables are informative for understanding the approach. Writing is generally clear with limitations and design choices (conservative rule induction, applicability constraints) explicitly discussed, though a few presentation gaps remain (e.g., no mention of code or hyperparameter reporting) that prevent it from being exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (interactive definitions, alignment-based chart extension, community results), making the flow from method to experiments easy to follow. Notation and examples appear consistent and the writing quality is good, with effective figures/tables implied by the clear reporting of user-facing metrics (240 structures, 74.7% naturalized utterances). Presentation could be improved by explicitly reporting implementation details and hyperparameters (code availability is not mentioned) and clarifying the conservative alignment induction conditions in more detail to aid reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusion, and its main contributions are stated early (interactive definitions + alignment-based chart extension). Notation appears consistent and the examples (voxel structures, user statistics) and figures/tables are informative for understanding the approach. Writing is generally clear with limitations and design choices (conservative rule induction, applicability constraints) explicitly discussed, though a few presentation gaps remain (e.g., no mention of code or hyperparameter reporting) that prevent it from being exceptional.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and practically useful idea—naturalizing a core programming language through user-supplied head/body definitions and a conservative alignment-based chart extension that transplants derivations—which differs materially from prior interactive semantic-parsing work (e.g., Wang et al. 2016 and earlier imitation/interactive frameworks). The recursive use of newly defined utterances as building blocks and the chart-transplant mechanism for paraphrase generalization are non-trivial operational contributions beyond straightforward interactive learning. While the approach builds on well-established interactive supervision and parsing technologies, the specific combination and conservative alignment strategy would likely inform experts and is clearly differentiated from prior work, though it stops short of a radical theoretical departure. | B(4): The paper advances a notable and practically meaningful idea—letting users ‘‘naturalize’’ a core programming language via head–body definitions and a conservative, alignment-based chart extension to induce paraphrase/generalization rules—which differs from prior interactive semantic-parsing work by operationalizing community-driven, compositional language growth. While the overall framing builds on established interactive supervision and semantic-parsing paradigms (e.g., Wang et al. 2016 and context-dependent semantic parsing work), the specific mechanism of transplanting derivations from definition bodies and allowing user-defined primitives to be reused compositionally is a non-trivial and well-differentiated technical contribution. The empirical demonstration of large-scale community use (240 complex structures; 74.7% naturalized utterances) further supports that the method goes beyond incremental tweaks to existing systems, though conservative induction and limited alignment scope temper claims of broad generality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful idea—naturalizing a core programming language through user-supplied head/body definitions and a conservative alignment-based chart extension that transplants derivations—which differs materially from prior interactive semantic-parsing work (e.g., Wang et al. 2016 and earlier imitation/interactive frameworks). The recursive use of newly defined utterances as building blocks and the chart-transplant mechanism for paraphrase generalization are non-trivial operational contributions beyond straightforward interactive learning. While the approach builds on well-established interactive supervision and parsing technologies, the specific combination and conservative alignment strategy would likely inform experts and is clearly differentiated from prior work, though it stops short of a radical theoretical departure.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper advances a notable and practically meaningful idea—letting users ‘‘naturalize’’ a core programming language via head–body definitions and a conservative, alignment-based chart extension to induce paraphrase/generalization rules—which differs from prior interactive semantic-parsing work by operationalizing community-driven, compositional language growth. While the overall framing builds on established interactive supervision and semantic-parsing paradigms (e.g., Wang et al. 2016 and context-dependent semantic parsing work), the specific mechanism of transplanting derivations from definition bodies and allowing user-defined primitives to be reused compositionally is a non-trivial and well-differentiated technical contribution. The empirical demonstration of large-scale community use (240 complex structures; 74.7% naturalized utterances) further supports that the method goes beyond incremental tweaks to existing systems, though conservative induction and limited alignment scope temper claims of broad generality.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper includes a direct comparison to prior interactive supervision work (Wang et al., 2016) but otherwise lacks breadth in baselines—important competing approaches in context-dependent semantic parsing and imitation/interactive learning (e.g., 2014 frameworks) are not evaluated. It is unclear whether comparisons were carried out under identical data/evaluation conditions or with matched hyperparameters and seeds, which limits fairness. The authors do discuss limitations of prior rule induction and the need for conservative extensions, but the empirical analysis does not fully isolate which components drive gains versus baseline behavior. Overall, comparisons are present but incomplete for a convincing, comprehensive evaluation against relevant recent methods. | B(4): The paper includes at least one reasonable baseline (the interactive supervision method of Wang et al. 2016) and situates itself relative to prior interactive semantic-parsing work, acknowledging limitations of its induction heuristics. However, comparisons appear limited to that single prior method and the summary does not indicate broader baselines from context-dependent semantic parsing or grammar-induction literature (e.g., related imitation/interaction frameworks circa 2014) that are highly relevant. It is also unclear from the summary whether all experiments used identical data and metrics for head-to-head fairness, though the paper does report comparable usage and task metrics and notes when prior methods degrade user experience. Overall the empirical comparison is good but not fully comprehensive against the most relevant competing approaches.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper includes a direct comparison to prior interactive supervision work (Wang et al., 2016) but otherwise lacks breadth in baselines—important competing approaches in context-dependent semantic parsing and imitation/interactive learning (e.g., 2014 frameworks) are not evaluated. It is unclear whether comparisons were carried out under identical data/evaluation conditions or with matched hyperparameters and seeds, which limits fairness. The authors do discuss limitations of prior rule induction and the need for conservative extensions, but the empirical analysis does not fully isolate which components drive gains versus baseline behavior. Overall, comparisons are present but incomplete for a convincing, comprehensive evaluation against relevant recent methods.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper includes at least one reasonable baseline (the interactive supervision method of Wang et al. 2016) and situates itself relative to prior interactive semantic-parsing work, acknowledging limitations of its induction heuristics. However, comparisons appear limited to that single prior method and the summary does not indicate broader baselines from context-dependent semantic parsing or grammar-induction literature (e.g., related imitation/interaction frameworks circa 2014) that are highly relevant. It is also unclear from the summary whether all experiments used identical data and metrics for head-to-head fairness, though the paper does report comparable usage and task metrics and notes when prior methods degrade user experience. Overall the empirical comparison is good but not fully comprehensive against the most relevant competing approaches.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a plausible interactive system and a usage-driven evaluation (240 structures built; 74.7% of recent utterances naturalized) and compares to a prior interactive supervision method, but the experiments are missing important controls. There are no reported hyperparameters, seeds, code, or runtime/memory figures to enable reproducibility, and ablation/sensitivity analyses isolating the contribution of the alignment-based chart extension and conservative induction decisions are not provided. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations are acknowledged (precision tradeoffs, scope of chart extension), but statistical analyses, broader baselines, and deeper failure analyses are lacking to fully support the claims. | B(3): The paper provides convincing real-world use evidence (240 community-built voxel structures; 74.7% of the last 10K utterances used the naturalized language) and a comparison to prior interactive supervision work (Wang et al. 2016), which supports the core claims. However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of conservative versus aggressive rule induction, scalability across candidate-action sizes) and reports no hyperparameters or code to enable reproduction. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations (selection infeasibility, conservative application of chart extension) are acknowledged, but the experimental analysis is not comprehensive enough to fully assess robustness and generality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a plausible interactive system and a usage-driven evaluation (240 structures built; 74.7% of recent utterances naturalized) and compares to a prior interactive supervision method, but the experiments are missing important controls. There are no reported hyperparameters, seeds, code, or runtime/memory figures to enable reproducibility, and ablation/sensitivity analyses isolating the contribution of the alignment-based chart extension and conservative induction decisions are not provided. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations are acknowledged (precision tradeoffs, scope of chart extension), but statistical analyses, broader baselines, and deeper failure analyses are lacking to fully support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides convincing real-world use evidence (240 community-built voxel structures; 74.7% of the last 10K utterances used the naturalized language) and a comparison to prior interactive supervision work (Wang et al. 2016), which supports the core claims. However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of conservative versus aggressive rule induction, scalability across candidate-action sizes) and reports no hyperparameters or code to enable reproduction. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations (selection infeasibility, conservative application of chart extension) are acknowledged, but the experimental analysis is not comprehensive enough to fully assess robustness and generality.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear main claims and provides empirical evidence (240 built structures; 74.7% naturalized utterances) and an implementation in SEMPRE, but the experimental methodology lacks critical details needed for reproducibility (no code/hyperparameters/seeds reported) and quantitative rigor (no statistical tests or error bars). The comparison to prior interactive supervision (Wang et al. 2016) is useful but appears limited—ablation studies isolating the chart-extension versus other components, and broader baselines, are missing. The conservative alignment-based extension and its restriction to single-utterance bodies are sensible but constitute unsupported assumptions about generality; overall the results are promising but there are methodological gaps that prevent full technical validation. | B(4): The paper’s main empirical claims are generally supported by the reported community deployment (240 structures) and usage statistics (74.7% of last 10K utterances) and the described conservative alignment-based chart extension, and it compares to a relevant baseline (Wang et al. 2016). There are no mathematical derivations to verify, and the presented algorithmic description appears consistent with the implementation choices noted (SEMPRE, conservative rule induction). However, the experimental methodology omits details that would affect reproducibility and strength of evidence (no code/hyperparameters reported, no statistical tests or ablation analyses described), and the alignment-based generalization is restricted to single-utterance bodies which limits the scope of the claim. Overall the technical approach is sound but some empirical validation and reporting gaps leave room for stronger verification.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear main claims and provides empirical evidence (240 built structures; 74.7% naturalized utterances) and an implementation in SEMPRE, but the experimental methodology lacks critical details needed for reproducibility (no code/hyperparameters/seeds reported) and quantitative rigor (no statistical tests or error bars). The comparison to prior interactive supervision (Wang et al. 2016) is useful but appears limited—ablation studies isolating the chart-extension versus other components, and broader baselines, are missing. The conservative alignment-based extension and its restriction to single-utterance bodies are sensible but constitute unsupported assumptions about generality; overall the results are promising but there are methodological gaps that prevent full technical validation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main empirical claims are generally supported by the reported community deployment (240 structures) and usage statistics (74.7% of last 10K utterances) and the described conservative alignment-based chart extension, and it compares to a relevant baseline (Wang et al. 2016). There are no mathematical derivations to verify, and the presented algorithmic description appears consistent with the implementation choices noted (SEMPRE, conservative rule induction). However, the experimental methodology omits details that would affect reproducibility and strength of evidence (no code/hyperparameters reported, no statistical tests or ablation analyses described), and the alignment-based generalization is restricted to single-utterance bodies which limits the scope of the claim. Overall the technical approach is sound but some empirical validation and reporting gaps leave room for stronger verification.

main_claims: Users can naturalize a core programming language by interactively providing definitions (head-body pairs) to extend the language for complex actions.
Interactive definitions plus an alignment-based chart extension enable the system to generalize paraphrases (e.g., infer that 'up' means 'top').
A community of users used the system to build 240 complex voxel structures, and usage of the naturalized language reached 74.7% of the last 10K utterances.
_summary.methodology: Seed a core programming language and let users incrementally define new utterances via definitions (head and body sequences of understood utterances); induce grammar rules from these definitions and conservatively extend the parsing chart by aligning spans in head and body to transplant derivations, implemented in SEMPRE.
baselines: Interactive supervision method of Wang et al. (2016)
metrics: Number of complex voxel structures built (240)
Percentage of utterances using the naturalized language (74.7% of the last 10K utterances)
limitations: Interactive selection from candidate lists is infeasible for large action spaces (e.g., >50 candidates).
Alignment-based chart extension is applied only when the definition body consists of one utterance (paraphrases), and is performed conservatively to avoid low-precision rules.
Low-precision induced rules significantly degrade user experience, motivating conservative rule induction.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - natural language interfaces / semantic parsing

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics who develops and evaluates semantic parsers and interactive learning systems in industry and academia.
traits: insists on clear, reproducible experimental procedure (datasets, code, hyperparameters, seeds)
requires quantitative baselines and ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each component
looks for rigorous evaluation metrics and statistical significance when claiming improvements
notices implementation details and scalability (runtime, memory, deployment constraints)
values error analysis that connects failure modes to specific design choices
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the validity of the experimental claims and whether the methodology, baselines, and ablations support the conclusions.

reviewer_b.name: Language-Interface & Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor researching natural language interfaces, semantic parsing, and human-in-the-loop language grounding, with a track record of work on program synthesis from language and community-facing systems.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior work on semantic parsing, grammar induction, and interactive learning
attends to the plausibility and scalability of the interaction model for real users and communities
values broader impact, usability, and whether the approach meaningfully advances natural language understanding or tooling
looks for convincing qualitative evidence (examples, user behavior) that supports claims about naturalization and emergent language
notices connections to related paradigms (e.g., programming by demonstration, crowd-sourced language evolution) and potential limitations
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the paper's novelty, fit within the research landscape, and the significance and realism of claimed impacts on natural-language programming interfaces.
